Hey guys, JB is in the house. Time for the weekly intensive training and as promised, this week's topic is going to be on tonality, but I'm also going to talk about overall unconscious communication. And the reason for that is because if you guys, if you're on the daily motivations, you probably noticed one of my videos this week, one of my trainings was a guy named Chancey who works for me, right? And I kind of had this little rip into shred session with his body language where he went to a seminar and this is why, you know, when someone doesn't really know what they're doing and they're teaching, it's very dangerous. He went to a seminar, I don't want to name the company, but they're obviously a bunch of idiots because they gave him knowledge without giving him the context of how to use the knowledge. And also the knowledge they gave was kind of stupid and had to do with dressing for a success and that classic mistake they made was they equated dressing for success with wearing a suit and tying. And that's just not, nothing to do with dressing for success is dressing up in a suit. Now in some industries, yes, that is dressing for success, but in most industries, it's not. All right? So I kind of went and ripped him apart on camera. This was, I think on Wednesday, maybe a Tuesday, Wednesday, last one this week, right? And then I went out and got him a new suit, got him a new shirt, the whole thing, and he showed how you could take someone from like just looking weird and off-putting to the point where you're never going to do business with them, how with a basic quick makeover, it took us like 15 minutes, right? The guy actually looks really, really good. All right? And maybe think that today, I don't want to just talk about tonality per se. I want to talk about tonality in the context of unconscious communication. So let me explain to you, I'm going to start from the beginning here, okay? And really explain, you know, unconscious communication versus conscious communication, how they work together, and also how you can quickly get to the point where you can use unconscious communication to, you know, dramatically increase your closing rate, like right now, very quickly, right? So let me quickly explain what I talk about unconscious communication, unconscious, right? Versus just conscious communication. When we talk about conscious, we typically think of, it's the words that we say. It's the words, it's the logical case that we make. It's how we get our point across to someone in their conscious mind. In other words, remember that the human brain is really, really powerful, right? It's immensely powerful. But our unconscious mind is infinitely more powerful than our conscious mind. Our conscious mind is that part of our brain that tells us what we should focus on, what's important. And then it can really capture maybe three to four percent of what's going on around us. For instance, right now, you're listening to the words that I say, right? Your eyes are seeing my face, you're zeroing it on me, right? Maybe depending on where you're sitting, if there's a very loud noise around you, you hear that, okay? If someone else is talking loudly, you'll hear that, right? If it's particularly cold or warm where you are, you'll notice that it's warmer, cold, right? But out, and maybe there's something in the background that's like right in your field of vision that you can't ignore. But what typically happens is, okay, is that because the conscious mind has a limited amount of processing power, it essentially deletes out everything that it sees, hears, touches, tastes and feel your five senses, right? It deletes it out so it's able to manage and make sense of what's important around you. In other words, your brain has dictated that, you know what? What's important right now is I need to understand exactly what you're saying and to think about those words, internalize those words. I want to watch him pick up his body language shoes, the whole thing. That's a conscious decision you've made, right? You're sitting down, hopefully your chair is comfortable. So after a couple of minutes of sitting, you're no longer aware even of the pressure that the chair is exerting on your legs. But it's still there. You're no longer aware of the temperature of the feeling of the air on your skin. You don't notice any of the lights that are on that you're sort of part of the background. In other words, what your brain does because your conscious mind has limited processing power, it makes a decision and that decision comes from a place called your RAS, your articular activating system. And this is that part of your brain that chooses what you should focus on. Okay. So right now you're focusing on me and just a couple of other things. The rest of the information from coming in from your five senses, your conscious mind says not important enough. I'm going to ignore that. However, it doesn't get lost. You see, while your conscious mind has a relatively limited amount of processing power, your unconscious mind has unlimited processing power. It can capture everything. I want you to think of it this way. Imagine like almost like you're your conscious mind, your RAM, your random access memory. It's what you can do right now to do tasks. It's like task oriented in the moment situation. So while you might have only a little amount of RAM on your computer, you could have a massive hard drive that stores all the information. That's a pretty good analogy of how the brain works. So while you are not consciously aware of every little thing around you, your unconscious mind picks it all up. It's literally, it's like millions and billions of times more powerful than your conscious. So watch. So while you're listening to me, your unconscious mind is busy working away over time, regulating your heartbeat, controlling your breathing, making sure that your blood vessels are delivering enough oxygen to the different parts of your body, making sure that your digestion system is functioning correctly. That is everything, all the autonomic functions in your body are completely being regulated without you even thinking about it. In other words, the conscious mind is what you're thinking about doing. I'm focusing on Jordan conscious decision. Your unconscious mind takes care of everything else. Make sense? Good. Now here's the deal. That into itself is not that profound. However, what makes this profound is when you start to realize that while you delete all this other information, your unconscious mind does not only capture it all, but it also organizes it and uses develop patterns of behavior. In other words, if I right now I'm going to go walk out as a door right there, right? When I walk out that door, right? I open the door and I simply step out, right? I've seen that door many, many times, right? Let's say I go to a strange door, door I've never seen before. I don't walk up to the door and say, hmm, that's a door. I wonder what's on the other side. Is it safe to go outside or is it a parallel universe outside? Is it going to be a tiger? Wait, my unconscious mind tells me based on your life experience and entering and exiting through doors that there's going to be a safe zone on the other side. It's simply a way for me to get from one room to another, from inside to outside. Without my unconscious mind, I would have to stop at every single door, every crack in the sidewalk. Everything I do would have to be for the first time. My unconscious mind turns things into patterns, which allows me to move through the world elegantly. You get the difference? So in those, I don't look at a door and say, hmm, what is that strange little mob there? I wonder what happens if I, I've never seen this particular knob before. I wonder what this knob does. No, my brain says, okay, this is a knob. You've seen four billion knobs throughout your life. This knob needs to be turned. It's not like every knob is the first knob I saw. Guess what? All of that stuff, all of those patterns, all of that instant decision making it, you don't think about it all comes from your unconscious mind. That's what all this is. It's not, you don't, you don't walk up to a door and say, let me, let me see if I should make, should I open this door or not? No, it's instantaneous. Your unconscious mind, it relieves your conscious mind of the task. So because it only has to work at all that shit, now your conscious mind can focus on what's important, that three or 4% that really, really matters. Like when you walk out that door, what exactly do you see? Okay. So your conscious mind is basically what's in front of you, what you're focusing on, your unconscious mind takes it, everything else and turns it into patterns of behavior that you can then act on. So when we talk about someone having a very high action threshold, we're talking about the beliefs they have about main, making buying decisions that, you know, I make good decisions quickly, or I should always think about things as another set of beliefs, or I should trust in general, I should trust salesman or salesman or not to be trusted. Those are patterns that your unconscious mind has developed over years and years based on everything that you've seen, everything that you've heard, whether it's on TV in person from a distance up close, it could be something as simple as you're watching, you're reading something else on TV in the corner, there was something about salesman being dishonest and some funny gag on TV about salesman not to be trusted. You could have been watching Glenn Gary, Glenn Ross, by the way, right? All that sales would not have to be trusted in their hard charging sales, even the Wolf of Wall Street, and you could have been a kid and that picked up something, got inserted in your unconscious mind and was the seed for not trusting salespeople. It could be something your parents whispered in your ear when you were six years old. It could be your brain saying, don't take candy, your mommy said, don't take candy from strangers, don't get in the car with someone you don't know, and your unconscious mind has extrapolated that and turned it into one giant pattern of don't trust anyone. See, what very often happens with our unconscious mind is that it develops patterns while we're very young and those patterns serve us while we're young. They protect us. They help us move through the world without getting in trouble, without, you know, walking off a cliff, without getting into the car with a potentially bad person, right? They protect us, not to take candy from strangers, right? The whole thing. But as an adult, the rules of the game change. See, now you're educated enough that your conscious mind has another experience that you kind of know when you see something, if it makes sense or not. That's conscious. Does it make sense or not? And you should act on it. Yet your unconscious mind is so powerful that it instantly predisposes you towards not taking action. This is why so many people have problems achieving success. It's not causing, I will join my programs in the early days. I would go out there and teach people how to close, how to sell, all the stuff you need to be successful on a sales side, right? I didn't teach them entrepreneurship, marketing, how to run a business, but I didn't address their belief systems. I didn't address the unconscious, their inner game. And because of that, some of them didn't achieve success. When I realized that, I said, Oh my God, I, if I really want to help people achieve massive success, I have to speak to both of their minds. I can't just teach someone a skill without essentially addressing the emotional baggage that emotional baggage they have in terms of patterns that don't serve them anymore. Like the parents said, money is the root of all evil. Or if you work really hard, you're going to ignore your family or you lose your relationship with God, or people won't like you. People will resent you, you lose your friends. There's all of these things get inserted in us when we're very young, and they form these patterns of behavior. And then they either serve us through our life and protect us, or they plague us. They stop us from taking action. They stop us from charging forward. And most importantly now, to bring it back to sales and persuasion, they're in every person you speak to, every single person you try to influence, every person you negotiate with, everyone you try to close, they are all being controlled by these unconscious patterns of behavior. Their unconscious mind gives them gut feels either are going to be, Yeah, I want to do it or not. I don't trust sales people. I got to think about things. So much of the objections that we hear. And the reticence that people have to taking actions they should be taking. Remember, I said this is a great clip that you say, you say, you know, it's about getting this. I said, listen, the straight line is powerful. And when you become proficient at it, you can get people to do things they shouldn't do and buy things they shouldn't buy. And I say, don't do that. I'm serious. Don't do that. What you want to use the straight line for is to help people do things they should be doing, take actions they should be taking, buy things they should be buying. But they're blocked in some way because of these patterns of behavior. So the patterns block them from getting the things they need and living the life they deserve. As a salesperson using the straight line system, by addressing the unconscious aspect of communication as well, we allow these people to get the things they need, the things that they want, things that will make their life better by helping them overcome those unconscious barriers and obstacles that exist in their mind. And they're not even fucking aware of them, they're taking action. That's why I'm able to close at such a high level. Why don't they do it? And then how do we use today? We're going to focus on this asset. How do we use unconscious communication to dramatically tip the odds of success in our favor? That's the frame for today. Okay, so that being said, let's talk about how we speak to both minds. So when it comes to the conscious mind, our primary way of communicating with the conscious mind is through words. We say things that make an airtight logical case, hopefully, right? Or some sort of logical case or attempt to make a logical case in order to show someone that what we're selling has value. So for instance, let's say I'm selling insurance, right? And I want to sell someone a life insurance policy. The conscious case that I make is that this policy makes sense for you to buy economically, you put an X number of dollars, right, which is a very low amount based on what you make as an inconsequential amount. And on the outside, the other side of the question, if something goes wrong, God forbid, right, you get X number of dollars back, and that money will then cover this expense, that expense and set the people that you love up for the future. So essentially make sense. But that's not enough. It's also better than the competition, meaning based on what other people charge for that same service, ours is a better has a cost benefit ratio, we have better features, we have better cancellation features, we allow you to borrow against it. In other words, it's the ABC. It's the words that you say, it's the case that you make about cost benefit ratio, the actual features and the benefits, logical certainty, the airtight logical case comes from the words that we say. And on the flip side of the equation, we have the unconscious communication that comes from our tonality and our body language. Now the subject of today is specifically tonality, but I don't want to just go through the 10 tonality, I've done that before and I'll touch on them, I'll keep doing that, we just keep doing it again and again, this is about constant never ending improvement. But the point is I want to give you a different perspective that's going to allow you to really understand what's going on and why this is so important. For instance, the other day I was training someone in my own company who doesn't have sales experience, he doesn't use a straight line. And I heard the guy out of the corner of my selling and I was like, it's like fingernails on a blackboard to me. And I'm saying, all right, well, what's so terrible? Well, number one, you know, the words were wrong. I caught his first page, the words were not correct, right? And even worse than that, because the words were incorrect, the tonality being applied, it was just so off that it just sounded horrific to me. And I realized that it's not just enough to explain someone the logical side, like it's important because you need to make this case. There's something else going on. And what it is is that when the words are in the correct order, it starts to make sense why you're applying certain tonalities. In other words, I always say, if you go back to the rules of prospecting, I say, one of the rules is every question you ask has its own specific tonality, a best tonality. And if you apply the right tonality, you get one response. And if you apply the wrong tonality, you get a very different response, right? When I watch something, I said, you know, when I need to go back a step here and really explain why this is so crucial. So what happens is the words, right, are making the airtight logical case. And your tonality and body language in person are speaking to the person's unconscious mind and hitting those patterns of behavior and giving them gut feelings about whether something makes sense or not. Should I keep listening or not? Can this person help me or not? It is not a logical decision they make. It's just to get a gut feeling. And we call that a gut feeling. But it happens up here based on everything you've seen, heard, read, experienced, whether it was directly or out of the corner of your eye, it forms these patterns and how you dress, how you speak, how you shake hands, how you make eye contact, either it serves to say, yup, this pattern says it's a person worth listening to. Well, the pattern says there's something going wrong here. I got to get out of the situation quick. So when I saw this guy work, Chancy, right, who's an intern here, right? And I see this kid and I take one look at him, I'm like, I'm like, there's something wrong there. The words, no matter what he said, he would never have a chance to show his stuff. Because my unconscious mind put out a warning bell and it said novice alert, novice alert, this person does not know what's going on, novice alert, don't talk, you get it? So when it comes to unconscious communication, it's a combination of how we dress, how we make eye contact, how we shake hands, how we move our body, how close we stand, the general feeling someone gets when they lay eyes on us for the first time. And the tonality is what we apply to the words that we say. Okay, so and together, those things make up this body of knowledge unconscious communication. When you think of tonality, and here's now where it gets interesting, mostly we talk about using tonality to keep someone interested, to keep someone hanging on the edge of their seat, to actually get our point across to speak to someone emotionally. So we know tonality is about creating emotion. So I said, you watch, hey, John, the reason for the course, the reason for the call, or say to you, John, this product is awesome, it's going to fill your needs, it will help you resolve all your problems. I said, John, this problem, this will help you fill your needs, it will resolve all your problems. I say it one way, you're like, whatever, I say it the other way, it has a very different effect. Right. So that's the basic form of tonality, right? You know, we say something, and it makes gives an emotional response. But there's a much, much higher level of tonality that I want to go through right now. It's got nothing to do with just the emotions got to do with taking control. And you've heard me say this many times about taking control of someone's monologue, their internal monologue. And this is basically the key to the whole thing. When you speak to someone, they look at you. And at the same time they look at you, they're like, uh-huh, uh-huh, their brain saying, that makes sense. This is stupid. This guy's a moron. Oh, wow, this guy's really sharp. Oh, that's good. Ooh, I want to hear more. They talk to themselves. People will, the brain multitasks, right? So remember, as you speak to someone, whether it's in person or over the phone, their brain is running away a mile a minute, narrating either for you against you. It's a silent narration that's running in the background. And what happens is unless you can take control of that internal narrative, it will start narrating heavily against you because the patterns that we've all developed since we're a kid are, don't trust, what does this person want to get from me? What do they want? What's their angle? This is how we're all built. Unless you're a really naive person, okay? There's that always even me with a low action. So there is that side of you that's always listening. Hmm. Let me think. What's this person to okay? Oh my God, what do they want to get in the air? Sure. I narrate. You can't help yourself unless the person uses a very high level of straight line communication. You see what happens when you use the straight line in a really high level, and you're layering on tonality at the tonality at the tonality adding person body language. What ends up happening is you lock up someone's unconscious monologue, that inner monologue, you lock it up and you take control of it. Does someone, every human being, imagine your prospect, right? They have an unconscious monologue, right? So you're talking, you say, Hey, John, how do you do it today? Like, oh my God, it's fucking salesman. What does this person want? You like, they like, and they say, okay, good. So they're talking to you and they're smiling and they're answering, but their brain inside is saying something different to themselves. They try, what's his angle? What's he's trying to say? They're like, they could have five things going on in their head as you're trying to influence them. But when you start applying the straight line tonalities, one after the next after the next, what ends up happening is you take control of someone's unconscious monologue, because you essentially lock it up. Watch what happens. Okay, it starts with literally the first words out of your mouth. So your intro, right? The reason I phrase my declarative, I phrase declarative as a question. This is one of your tonalities. One of the 10 tonalities. What does that mean? So when I start off a conversation, I say, hi, my name is Jordan Belford, calling from XYZ company. Now, watch again. Hi. Hi, my name is Jordan Belford. I'm calling from XYZ company in New York. How are you today? And when I ask a question, I raise my voice and think, how are you today? And I go, that's just how we communicate in this language and every language has a very similar pattern. So we say our declarations one way, we make statements one way, and we ask questions another way. I don't say how you're doing today. I say, how you're doing today? I use a question like tonality. So when I say, Hey, my name is Jordan Belfort. That is the expected way. That's the expect. Hi, my name is Jordan Belfort statement calling from XYZ company statement in Los Angeles. And I say, how are you today? That's the expected tonality. One will hear, they think they'll hear it when you pick up the when you when you answer the phone. So because expected, the what happens is the unconscious mind, okay, is like, Oh my God, here we go. So what's going on? Another salesman. Oh my God, right. But when you flip this upside down on its head, and you phrase a declarative as a question, here's what happens. It now looks like this. Hi, my name is Jordan Belfort. Hey, it's Jordan Belfort calling from XYZ company in New York. How you doing today? Uptown, uptown, they're like, what? Who is this? It sends their mind into search mode. So watch what's there. So not really think this through for a second. When someone raises their voice like that, and speaks in question like tones, what would you be thinking? You think, who do I know this person? Let me go Jordan Belfort. Does that ring the bell? Well, and then going even deeper, all the stuff that you don't actually say, but your unconscious mind is collapsing as well. I would hate to be out of the loop here, because that would make me look stupid. Or I know it's impolite. Because my mother told me I'm supposed to remember people I speak to more than once. And obviously, based on what this guy just said, using that tonality, I guess I'm supposed to know this guy. All of that, it's all verb reading in someone's brain. Seriously. That is precisely what happens when you phrase something as a question in the beginning. It's supposed to be a statement. They're like, what? That's what happens. Okay. So what do they do now? It's all unconscious. They're not saying those words to themselves. They're unconscious mind that has been developed over x 30 years, 40 years, 20 years of life is throwing out all this stuff into their conscious mind. And what happens is tonality, tonality, tonality. Guess what they're not doing? They're not narrating against you anymore. What they're doing instead now is they're trying to keep up with all the unspoken words they're hearing for your tonality. In other words, you say how you're doing today. Those are a few words. But by saying how you're doing today, the words that they also hear are I really want to know. They hear extra words. Like when you say not john, the reason for the call. And what you've also said was, Now, john, this is really important. You get now listen to this, john. Don't tell anyone. They cannot help but get those feelings inside from tonality because your tonality is not speaking to this part of their mind. That's not who you're speaking to. You're speaking to this part of the person's mind. You speak to their unconscious mind and their unconscious mind is made up of strictly patterns. It's not logic. It's patterns, snap reactions, gut feelings, instant decisions. While your words are being analyzed by their conscious mind, which has very little processing power relatively. So all they can think about consciously, right, is that, Okay, I'm hearing what he said. And I speak this language, right? So I'm okay. Yeah. And any extra bit of processing power left is completely obliterated by the extra words they're hearing and the thoughts and feelings they have based on the patterns you've hit. So you're addressing their patterns with your unconscious communication. So it's double communication. You're communicating consciously and unconsciously at the same time, using your words to speak to their conscious mind and your tonality and body language to speak to their unconscious mind. This is why we look at certain people and we just want to, I want to do business with them. I like them. They makes, it's a pattern based on what I've seen throughout my life. The chances are really high that if someone that looks like this, talks like this, it acts like this is probably so worth listening to. So we tip the odds dramatically in our favor by focusing on our unconscious communication as well as our conscious communication. We focus on both. So let's keep going here. And like your presupposing tonality is like, you can using that again, I can't believe how powerful it is. Like it sounds so good. Like this is one tonality. And it's so good. It's like, it's almost like a throwaway tonality. People think it's cool. I think it's number eight or nine. We'll get to it. Right. But I'll go, I want to go through it first. It's when it gets neglected, this tonality, right? It's cool. Let's use number nine. It's cool. You're a presupposing tone. It's when you're in the latter part of a sale, and you, you're making your airtight logical case. And let's say it's going to be that, let's say you're trying to let's let's use the seminar world, right? Because it was in the case of a seminar world. And so when I was explaining to someone how, listen, so, you know, we're going to do this whole thing. And, you know, you can sell the tickets and you'll sell out the room, right? But on top of that, there's also this other stuff happening because these people love to buy products and there's money to be made there. And as we can come back down the road and do even more and develop code. So I was trying to so the initial sale was with someone who wants to do a live event with me in a certain country. Let's just pick, let's just pick Singapore. Okay, so I want to live Jordan, Belford event in Singapore, right? So when someone is talk when a promoter is talking to me about that, right, what's their big concern? Well, if I do a live event in Singapore, I want to make sure that I sell out the room. Right? That's what I'm going to talk about that. Right? Yeah, well, if you if you pay me my fee, and you advertise my name because it's the brand and because people need to learn how to sell, you'll be able to sell enough tickets, not just break even, you'll make a lot of money. Right? But what I end up doing is I go way beyond just taking that conversation about, yeah, you'll fill the room. What I saw say is, do you listen, obviously, you'll fill up the room, but beyond that. So I use this tone out and I'm like, Yeah, obviously, yeah, we'll fill the room up. But more importantly, what happens beyond that is all the products people are going to buy going back year after year, you're building a business for the long term that can really make you rich. In other words, what I've done is I talk about the events in like, it's a foregone conclusion. I say, you'll make money with the event. But besides that, on top of that, it's much bigger than that. That's just the beginning. Like, so what I by doing that, I essentially take the question of success or failure out of the picture for gankers. I say, Yeah, you'll fill the room. But more importantly, let's talk about the ancillary benefits. You'll also get this, you'll get that, you'll get that. That's your presupposing tone. And I guess, you know, until Dan pointed that out, I was like, it's so in second digit, Mike, he kept saying, dude, it was so amazing. Like, I want like, you could just see how the conversation was like, you was like, you went, you're like, you were check, like, check, it's done, like, it's over. It's a success. You took it, you future paste the conversation past what you were even selling the guy and say, Okay, you already bought that you succeeded. Now it's all about what happens next. And that presupposing tone, which is ignored by most people is so fucking powerful. Because remember what you've done here is like, I, I, I said, I got, yeah, you know, listen, you'll fill the event up. That's not even the issue here. Let's talk past that. Let's talk about how you make real money over the long term. And I moved the conversation past what I'm even selling the person using this presupposing tone. That makes sense. Let's talk about really make money over the long term coaching program. And I'm dead serious. Like I'm not making any of this up. In other words, I'm not lying to the guy. And I'll be interacting I'll be training Scarcity, basically, people want more of what they perceive there to be less of. Human nature, we live in a scarcity mentality, right? So you create scarcity, it's lowering your voice, and John, the reason for the call today. Informational scarcity. John, reason for the call, John. Here's the deal, lower the voice. Just, it's not a whisper, it's whisper with a bit of, John, reason for the call. So it's not John, the reason for the call. That's a whisper, whisper. This is a power, I call it a power, and make sure you get this, it's called a power whisper. It's a whisper with a bit of oomph in there. Like, John, reason for the call. You see the energy, you see like, it's like, I'm not like John, reason for the call. It's not like I'm losing my power. I'm keeping my power, and I'm even have some enthusiasm. Like, there's enthusiasm embedded in my scarcity. I'm gonna try and reason for the call today. Something just came across. So there's a bit of excitement, but you'll lower your voice, and that's the tonality of scarcity, okay? And remember this three types. The words is called verbal scarcity, right? John, this is in very short supply. That's verbal, the words. John, this is in very short supply, or total scarcity, add on to it. John, this is in very short supply. You add the tonality of scarcity on to the words. And then you have informational scarcity, which is, John, this is in very short supply, and no one even knows it yet. Even the information is scarce. Those are your three levels of scarcity, okay? Next, you have the tonality of, I really wanna know. That's just basically what I call it. I really wanna know, right? So I said to you, John, how you doing today? Not, how you doing today? So I said to you, how you doing today? You're like, you know that I know, that I know that you know, that I don't give a shit how you doing today, and you don't care how I'm doing it. It's a perfunctory greeting I give just to be polite, because my mommy told me to do that. Versus, how you doing today? And you're like, well, that's not how I usually get to like, okay, and that comes on the heels of what? Phrasing your declarative as a question. Hey, John, Jordan Belfort, calling from XYZ Company in LA. How you doing today? They're like, okay, what the fuck is going on? And then you own them, and I don't mean that in a bad way. I don't mean that you're gonna hurt them to get out of there, but they're in your magnetic zone. And when you're in person, you'll look good, you make that good handshake, they're right. I can't think they're like fuck, this guy's not your average bear. And you open up the possibility for influence. So there's, without that, without this, what happens? The person, they shut down. They try to take control of the conversation. And once that happens, guess what? You can't run any patterns. You're reacting. You're not being proactive, you're being reactive. The only way you can use the straight line is to be proactive. You take control of the sale, and you use that control to start asking questions. But we take control through these tonalities. And by taking control of their unconscious mind along, stopping it from marrying against you, make it narrate for you, and get them hanging on your every word, and use that to do the right thing by people. Not to fuck them over, but to help them get things they need. See, I didn't forget, certainties are very much, you guys see my smiles, keep going. And then lastly, mystery and intrigue. Mystery and intrigue, is that what you say to someone, and I used it as an example, the reason for the call, and so it's a little different than scarcity is more like when you lower your voice, like, you know, John, now, right now, so there's only, you know, we only have X number of these to go, all right, there's only five days to go, that's scarcity versus mystery and intrigue, you say, you know, John, reason for the call, that's when you have that like, reason, you're hanging with that R, you're rolling it, the reason for the call, some mystery, you're creating intrigue, and what it does, it makes them press the phone closer, they just wanna know, it's like not quite scarcity, the reason like, you know, so I sort of divide between scarcity urgency, right, so scarcity is about urgency, mystery and intrigue is about keeping them interested, hanging on your every word, okay, and we use these 10 tonalities, not just to keep them interested as we talk, remember, in the beginning especially, it's about capturing their attention, taking control of their unconscious monologue, stopping it from narrating against you and get it to narrate for you, when you can do that, okay, it's easy to do, when you do that, you'll notice that everything else becomes easy, because they stop saying, oh fuck, watch this, who's this, they stop acting like assholes and start treating you with the respect you deserve, that's the key, and with that and that, let's open up to our questions and let's rock and roll. So this one's from Adam, for a two-call system, do you open the appointment conversation the same way as a cold call? So if you read the book, way of the wolf you probably have, you just forgot, right, in way of the wolf, I talk about this whole thing, about the difference between the two-call system and a one-call system, is that in a two-call system, you're entering into the conversation a second time, you're further down the straight line and you already have some gather some intelligence, right? So what you wanna do is a second call starts with like, hey John, if you're a cold, we spoke last week and I sent you out some information via email, okay, Zach ring a bell, not did you receive it, does Zach ring a bell? So I'll say, no, I didn't receive it, then you start with a no, I say, well, the reason for the call today, John, okay, is whatever it is, right, and then I'll say, okay, real quick, let me just, now last time we spoke and I'll quickly go through the, I won't go through all the questions, I go through a very, very, an abbreviated intelligence gathering, where I just sort of confirm what they said last time and use that to get back into tight rapport again, right? Because remember, when you ask those questions the first time, you use it as an opportunity to get into deep rapport through active listening. So what I'll do now is I go through those questions again, but very quickly and I'm like, aha, you're up, and I said, now last time we spoke, you said that X, Y, Z was that, is that right, right? Aha, and he'll start talking again and use it to get back into rapport. But a second call will typically, if imagine, let's say your initial call was six minutes in a two call system, to get to the point, you said, great, let me send you out the information and we'll meet again, right? But we'll talk again. If it was six minutes in your opening call, it should be two and a half to three minutes in your second call. So you condense that portion, use it to get back into tight rapport and then go into your presentation, okay? That's the answer. So this is from Peter. I'm a house painter, does tonality work when you are talking to a client that tells you that from the beginning he's getting three quotes? I keep hearing this, okay, this whole issue about multiple quotes and does the straight line, and here's the deal buddy, okay? If everyone is quoting $98, you're quoting $150, there's no tonality in the world that's gonna save the day. Flat out. The straight line works when everyone's quoting around the same price and they wanna go with you because of all the other intangibles. So maybe the straight line system and influence and persuasion, maybe they'll give you a 10%, 20% leeway. So maybe you'll have to be 10% higher than someone else, but you sound so good, you make them so confident and you even do that presupposing, oh my God, on top of the paint, also help patch all the cracks and this and that and try using your presupposing tonality to stack on extra benefits. But it's not gonna close a gap of $50 or $100 on a $50 job. So if your job is 100 and everyone's charging 100, you're charging 175, there's no tonality in the world that's gonna get you that job buddy, unless they don't care about money. We're talking about when you're all around the same price and you use your tonality, your buy-in, and also the words that you say to make sure that your service stands out and they trust you and they trust the company that stands behind you, and that's what this, so it's not like this magic solution for charging extra. We just be clear about that. But I promise you that if you have every, it's just three or four people around the same price, even if you're $5 more, whatever it might be, you use the right tonality, buy it, use your right, not just that, everything, the straight line, you will be the winner. Not every time, but more often than not, you'll outperform everyone around you because they remember there's the service you're providing and then how you explain the service you're providing. You go in there and say, I'm gonna paint your house and I go in there and do my thing and say, I'm gonna do this, I'm gonna do that, and I come up with a, spend some time explaining why your service is better, why you perform at a higher level, and you will see that, A, it gives you an extra bump on top of what they can charge more importantly, don't try to use this to charge more than ever around you. Try to come in at the same level or maybe just slightly higher and use the win more of the jobs. Okay, next? Someone's talking upstairs, I don't know who it is, just keep them quiet. All right. So keeping the powder dry is about keeping the ammunition for some position correct? Partially, my friend, keeping your powder dry has to do with many things. That's to do with number one, that there's only so much you can move someone in any one shot. Like you know when you have a car and you have gears, it goes, and it has that back off where like the car's gonna stop, pause, and then go forward. Someone doesn't go from a four to a 10 in one shot. If you try to go from, take them from a four to a 10, all they do is get overwhelmed. Someone can only take so much information at once. So you frame your case, you keep your powder dry. That's one side of it. Another side is that what I don't wanna do is get, say everything about my product and service, right? And then I'm done and they wanna think about it. And I've left myself with nothing great to change their models. What I'd rather do is I'm not saying don't make an amazing case in the first go, I want you to, make an excellent airtight case, but frame it. It's powerful, but it's framed. When they say let me think about it, and you say well let me ask you, does the idea make sense to you? Does it make sense, right? You now have other even more powerful benefits to start beginning your first loop with. It's not just about presupposing. Presupposing is part of that. But there's many reasons why you keep your powder dry. To avoid overwhelm, to avoid being a talking head, to leave yourself with more benefits for the back end. You get it, it's not so cut and dried. I don't know anything about that. What I do know is that if you signed up for this program and you're willing to go the extra mile, if you're willing to invest in yourself and learn and grow yourself, as long as you're not selling something that's a dead-end sales job, dude, you're gonna make it in this industry. You will, because everyone who studies the straight line does, bottom line, the system works. As long as you apply it and work hard. So you're in the right place. So this is from Bryce. Question. If we feel like we have a general idea of the SLB, but want to take it to the next level, why don't we really become a master of it? Well, Bryce, I tell you, again, I have a program called Straight Line Certification. It's a very formal program. And the reason it's taking me a long time to launch is because I want this to be something that is so amazing and so airtight. And part of that is something called interactivity, meaning that in the old way of training online, you watch me on video and you take notes, then you implement here with this new with Straight Line Certification, which is actually a five-tiered program. It's not just online. It's in-person training with me directly. We have an event where you actually show up and I train you personally, right? But with the certification, there's every few minutes, I'll ask you a question about a topic that we're learning and you'll respond. You can say yes, no, there's a number or something. And based on your response, there's a learning algorithm and it routes you to different contents that allows the train now to be fluid and interactive. When you do that, two things happen. Number one, it increases engagement because there's no longer me just talking while you listen. And I'm a very interesting person. I am, and I try to be, make sure you use my tonality to keep you engaged. But when you start being able to interact with me, engagement goes through the roof. Second, it allows you to go through the training more quickly or more slowly based on what you need. Everyone's training is slightly different based on how you answer questions. So if you answer a question incorrectly, I will loop back and give you more information on a topic and then ask you that question again till you get it right. So that's why, and just so you know, technically that's not very easy just for the software. I have to take a million different scenarios. So it's taking the longer than I thought, but our plans are is to formally launch this program on February 1. Now I've said to people, if you wanna be part of this early group, you can email me at JB at JordanBelfort.com. I've signed up some people, but not many. Anything out of the ordinary. If you're extraordinarily anything, then it puts you out of the norm. But there's ways to cut me, you really fat, really tall, really short, really this, you know, covered in pimples, whatever, albino white, you know, dark to the point of people, anything that makes people say, well, this is just, so that's not good, but it's not enough to stop you. You see, there are rules for body language. There are ways to wrap your package, to what you wear, to how you make eye contact, to how you shake hands, to how far you, close you stand to someone. You're gonna have to spend, what I want you to do, is I want you to spend extra time learning the rules of body language. So before I tell you to go out into the field and just say, just go out and do it, let me first set you up for success. I want you to master the art of body language first in a controlled environment for the next week or so. And then once you actually possess the skills, then let's go out into the field, let's just, let's step two. But what happens very often is that the reason someone has a limiting belief about this is because they weren't naturally adept at body language. So when they were in person, they did turn people off. Maybe they didn't look, make eye contact the right way. Maybe they didn't shake hands, maybe they stood too close. And because of that, they felt the disconnection with people. So they developed these limiting beliefs about themselves. They were actually correct. They sucked at it. But here's the thing, body language is a learnable skill. I can teach you how to be very effective in front of people. But I want to get you there empirically first in reality before I then make you over. So I don't want to sing out there without the knowledge and you'll just get blown away again. So first brush up on your body language and then let's talk about getting out there and overcome what's up here. Okay. Next one's from Andres. So with the two-call system, do you always set up a follow-up appointment? And if you do, what's your process as they don't show up? So if you don't set up a follow-up appointment, how do they, by nature of a two-call system, you're setting up a second appointment, right? So here's the thing, there's two ways to do this, right? In a two-call system, and here's why I can't answer this, because there's too many variables, right? There's two-call systems where the first call is on the phone, and then you follow up with an in-person appointment. There's other two-call systems where you call on the phone and you follow up with the phone. There's other ones where you're first one's in person and you follow up in person, or you follow up on the phone. There's lots of variables here, okay? So I can't answer that. What I will tell you is I've struggled with back and forth, and the only way to figure this out is to test it in your industry, is do you want to confirm an appointment with someone? And I always start off saying no. I do not want to confirm my appointments because if someone doesn't show up, then they're gonna feel that bad about them. Oh, me, that sets me up for the next call. But if your cancellation rate's really, really high, then I would start confirming. You have to test this stuff. I choose not to confirm, because it gives someone a chance to say, oh, no, I'll cancel. If you get someone, here's what happens a lot with it, and this is, let's say, two-call on the phone, where you speak to someone on Monday, set up a call for Friday, but they forgot by Friday. And you call them, you'll catch them or you won't. If you catch them, they'll take the call. Or if they have to reschedule, then they'll reschedule if you're a bit of a get-in-the-next-time. So I choose not to do the day before the check, because they'll just say, oh, no, it's a bad, let's reschedule. Too many cancellations that way. So I would start off not doing a call the day before, but if you gotta drive eight hours somewhere, well, I'd definitely rethink that. So there's been a lot of chicken involved as well. It's in those first couple of seconds when they lay eyes on you and they hear your voice, they're gonna make the decision. Is this a person worth listening to or not? The difference is you gotta be that much better because there's a lot of other options beside you versus when you're at someone's door or you're the only game in town for that moment. Here, it's not like that. So again, brush up on your skill sets, okay? Make sure you have your presentation, especially your first five or 10 seconds. I want that scripted. Your words, speak to the person's conscious mind. Your tonality and body language speak to their unconscious, their emotional mind. Logic and emotion, powerful combination.
